perturbation.original_text: (b) Payment Terms. Except as set forth in such Part 3 of Exhibit "A", payments from Co-Host to NAI shall be due thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Payment Terms. Except as set forth in such Part 3 of Exhibit "A", payments from Co-Host to NAI shall be due sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms from 30 days to 60 days introduces a potential delay in payment that could violate California's prompt payment laws, particularly if the agreement involves services where shorter payment timelines are mandated.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5 requires specific payment schedules for home improvement contracts. If this agreement were to apply to a home improvement project, mandating a 60-day payment period would likely conflict with the prescribed payment terms and schedules outlined in the statute. This section governs how payments should be scheduled based on the progress of the work, contradicting a flat 60-day term.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 7159.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 3. PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term from 30 to 60 days, potentially conflicting with California's prompt payment laws, especially for home improvement contracts. BPC 7159.5, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies payment schedules for home improvement contracts, indicating that payment terms should align with the progress of the work, which may not be compatible with a flat 60-day payment period. Therefore, changing the payment term to 60 days creates a contradiction if the agreement falls under home improvement contracts as defined in BPC 7151.2. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation reinforce this point, highlighting the conflict with mandated payment schedules, thus creating an inconsistency and a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (h) Any notices and demands provided hereunder must be in writing and will be deemed given upon the earlier of actual receipt or two (2) days after being sent by overnight Federal Express or Express Mail, return receipt requested, to the appropriate address set forth above, as such contacts and addresses may be changed by written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: (h) Any notices and demands provided hereunder must be in writing and will be deemed given upon the earlier of actual receipt or seven (7) days after being sent by overnight Federal Express or Express Mail, return receipt requested, to the appropriate address set forth above, as such contacts and addresses may be changed by written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deemed notice period from two days to seven days after mailing via overnight delivery could potentially violate certain California laws that require shorter notice periods for specific legal actions or contract terminations. This extended period might not provide sufficient time for the receiving party to respond within legally mandated deadlines.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 addresses service by mail and extends response times based on the method of delivery. While it adds time for mail service, it does not allow for a flat 7-day extension when using overnight delivery. If a legal notice requiring a response within a specific timeframe is sent by overnight mail, a 7-day delay in deeming it 'given' could prejudice the recipient's legal rights, especially since CCP 1013(c) only allows two calendar days to be added if the service is made by express mail.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The change from two days to seven days for deemed notice after overnight delivery contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013. While the snippet discusses extensions for mail service, it does not support extending the notification period to seven days when express mail is used. The Law Explanation points out that CCP 1013(c) only allows two calendar days to be added if the service is made by express mail. The altered contract language undermines the recipient's ability to respond within legally mandated timeframes, creating an inconsistency and potential legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: Late payments shall bear interest at the lesser of: (i) the maximum rate permitted by law, and (ii) the rate of 1.5% per month from the due date until paid.
perturbation.changed_text: Late payments shall bear interest at the rate of 2.0% per month from the due date until paid.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the late payment interest rate to a flat 2.0% per month might exceed the maximum allowable interest rate under California law for certain types of transactions, particularly if the agreement is considered a loan or involves consumer credit. California law sets limits on interest rates to prevent usury.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution sets the maximum interest rate for loans or forbearance of money, goods, or things in action primarily for personal, family, or household purposes at 10% per annum, or 5% over the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's discount rate. A flat 2.0% per month (24% annually) would likely violate this provision if the underlying transaction falls under the scope of this constitutional limit, unless an exemption applies (e.g., for certain financial institutions or licensed lenders).

perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the late payment interest rate to a flat 2.0% per month, potentially exceeding the maximum allowable interest rate under California law. The Law Explanation points to Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution, which sets a maximum interest rate that this change would likely violate if the transaction falls under its scope. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 mainly shows the California Codes, Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces the fact that the California Constitution Article XV addresses usury, strengthening the argument that the modified interest rate contradicts established legal limits.
